# Lesson — Sales — 2026-07-08

**Trigger:** First real-world operational input (Izzah's CS deck) landed
alongside Business's B2C/B2B tracker analyses, all pointing at the same
root cause from different angles: capacity and jobdesk clarity, not
strategy or lead supply.
**Source:** Internal (virtual staff feedback, distilled from a real CS
officer's own account) plus escalation-outcome context (Barry's prior
decisions on `ESC-20260706-BUS-01/02/03`).
**Lesson:** When multiple upstream findings all recommend "just execute
the existing plan," check who is actually available to execute before
sequencing the work, otherwise the plan looks Tier 1 (Sales can just do
it) when it is really Tier 3 (needs a real staffing decision first). Real
operational detail from an actual staff member surfaces this faster than
tracker data alone.
**Applies to:** any future execution ask that lands on the B2C CS/Sales
or B2B Sales function, check current real headcount and training state
before committing to a timeline.
**Status:** Logged.

# Lesson — Sales — 2026-07-08

**Trigger:** Izzah's account of the NAY-campaign freelance CS hire.
**Source:** Internal (virtual staff feedback, real-world account).
**Lesson:** Ad hoc freelance CS support without a named trainer and a
minimum onboarding runway costs more than it returns, freelancers
without product knowledge cannot activate accounts or handle
post-activation questions, so the core CS agent ends up doing that work
anyway on top of onboarding the freelancer.
**Applies to:** any future CS surge-staffing decision (campaign periods,
seasonal spikes). Logged to `ESC-20260708-SALES-02` as a policy ask.
**Status:** Logged.

departments.lessonsLearned:
# Business — Lessons Learned

Append-only log. Use `templates/lessons-learned.template.md` for each
entry. Newest entries first. Consolidate durable lessons into
`playbook.md` monthly.

---

<!-- Add entries below -->

# Lesson — Business — 2026-07-10

**Trigger:** Filling the "Accreditation" field in the DBS Foundation Grant
docx, `find_para(cell, "-short description-")` matched and then
overwrote a paragraph whose full text was actually
`"Startup Studio Indonesia\n-short description-"`, a label and a
placeholder joined by a line break inside one Word paragraph, not two
separate paragraphs. The label got wiped out along with the placeholder
and wasn't caught until a later pass.
**Source:** Self-observed.
**Lesson:** When scripting docx edits with python-docx, a `\n` inside
`paragraph.text` usually means a manual line break within a single
paragraph object, not a paragraph boundary. Before doing a substring match
to replace placeholder text, check whether the matched paragraph's full
text contains other content that must be preserved, and if so, edit only
the specific substring rather than clearing the whole paragraph.
**Applies to:** Any future docx-editing script, this repo or elsewhere.
**Status:** Logged.

# Lesson — Business — 2026-07-08

**Trigger:** Analyzing `reg_from_DB (2).csv`, the full registrant
database export (72,905 rows).
**Source:** Self-observed.
**Lesson:** 29,068 of the 72,905 rows (40%) were junk placeholder
records, identifiable by a hardcoded `year_reg` of 1899 (a null-date
sentinel value) with no username, no first-activity date, and zero
sessions. Taking the raw row count at face value would have overstated
the registrant base by 66% and understated the true lifetime conversion
rate. Always check for sentinel/default values (1899, 1970, 0, empty-string
defaults) in date fields before trusting a raw row count from a DB export,
especially one this much larger than a previously-seen figure for the
same population (this export's 72,905 vs. Revenue MoM's own cumulative
~43,324 registered accounts was the tell that something was off).
**Applies to:** Any future pull from `reg_from_DB` or similar direct
database exports, as opposed to hand-maintained trackers.
**Status:** Logged.

# Lesson — Business — 2026-07-06

**Trigger:** Analyzing the B2B pipeline tracker (`Pipeline B2B` sheet) and
B2C churn cohorts (`potential_churn_july2026` etc.) for the B2B/B2C
strategy pass.
**Source:** Self-observed.
**Lesson:** Two recurring tracker-hygiene patterns blocked cleaner
analysis and should be watched for in future pulls: (1) closed-won
records disappearing from a pipeline sheet instead of being marked won,
which makes win rate uncomputable from the sheet alone, seen in `Pipeline
B2B` where zero rows show Onboarding = True despite real historical B2B
revenue existing; (2) two tracking columns disagreeing with each other,
seen in `potential_churn_july2026` where 4 rows are marked "Converted"
while "1st contact" is False for all of them. When a work product hits
either pattern, flag it explicitly rather than picking one column to
trust silently, per the work-product rubric's "numbers reconcile"
criterion.
**Applies to:** Any future pull from `Pipeline B2B`, `subs_temp`, or the
churn-cohort sheets in `B2C Sales Tracker`.
**Status:** Logged.

# Lesson — Business — 2026-07-06

**Trigger:** First KPI dashboard pass since department stood up 2026-07-05.
Both Business KPIs had blank target/current/status rows in
`org/kpi-dashboard.md`, and neither sub-metric breakdown matched the full
set named in `business/SYSTEM-PROMPT.md` (pricing benchmarking and market
sizing were missing from Market & competitor analysis; signal-to-decision
lead time and recommendation-to-execution hit rate were missing from
One-step-ahead direction).
**Source:** Self-observed.
**Lesson:** A KPI with no sub-metric rows reads as "no status" instead of
"OFF TRACK." Always break a KPI into its full named sub-metrics
immediately on setup, even before any real data exists, so the dashboard
reflects a true gap rather than an empty cell. Also: `data-inbox/business/`
has never received a feed from Barry, so competitor/pricing/market-sizing
work is currently blocked on either (a) Barry feeding real data, or (b)
Business Evaluation Specialist starting public desk research as an
interim Tier 1 substitute.
**Applies to:** Quarterly competitor review cadence, weekly KPI dashboard
updates.
**Status:** Logged.

findings.headline: Two priorities this quarter: B2C's growth constraint is trial activation, not price or churn messaging, 88% of lost signups barely touched the product. B2B's constraint is throughput on an already-sourced pipeline, not lead supply.

sections.title: B2C: Trial activation (highest priority)
findings: Lifetime trial-to-paid conversion is 5.0% across 43,837 real registrants (junk placeholder rows excluded).
88.3% of everyone who never converted completed 0-2 sessions out of a 7-day trial, 56.3% completed zero sessions ever.
Only 2.5% of non-converters actually finished something close to a full trial and still said no, for the other 97.5% the product was never really evaluated.
recommendations: Build and test a Day-0 activation nudge, the existing nurture playbook starts at Day 2.
Sample-contact a small batch of zero-session registrants to learn the real reason before building a bigger campaign on a guess.
Hold off increasing discount-led acquisition spend until activation is fixed, that spend currently fills a funnel that loses 88% of entrants before price or product quality ever come into play.

sections.title: B2C: Discounting and churn execution
findings: ARPPU fell 24% (Rp140,064 to Rp105,860) from Dec 2025 to Jun 2026 while discount cost rose from a ~20% floor to 39-53% of gross revenue.
Lifetime blended discount rate confirmed twice at roughly 36% across two separate data pulls, 4,399 and 5,852 charge records.
A fully written churn-prevention playbook sits at 0% logged execution against a 656-record at-risk backlog, one cohort already a month stale.
11 records use a 100%-off discount code tagged as marketing/KOL talent barter deals, not customer discounts, mixed into the same field as real discounts.
recommendations: Review the discount ladder before any further discount-led acquisition push.
Run the existing churn playbook against the backlog, oldest cohort first.
Separate marketing-barter deals from customer discounts in the discount_type taxonomy.

sections.title: B2B: Pathway throughput (X/Y/Z)
findings: 126 of 132 sourced schools (95%) and 72 of 102 teacher signups (70%) have never been contacted or actioned, the worked pipeline is a fraction of what's already sourced.
Path Y (teacher outreach, foundation-funded) is 59% of active volume but has zero conversions past First Meeting.
Path X (school pays direct) is the only revenue pathway on the tracker and is 50% Cold or Stalled.
Path Z (principal outreach, foundation-funded) is the smallest pathway but has the best hit rate, 2 of 3 leads Hot.
Zero pipeline rows show Onboarding = True despite real historical B2B revenue existing, closed-won deals are not tracked forward.
recommendations: Freeze net-new Path Y outreach for one cycle, triage the 30 already in pipeline first.
Re-engage the 5 Path X leads marked Stalled with a fixed follow-up cadence.
Deliberately grow Path Z volume given its early hit rate.
Add a Closed Won status so pathway win rate becomes computable next cycle.

departments.lessonsLearned:
# HR & GA — Lessons Learned

Append-only log. Use `templates/lessons-learned.template.md` for each
entry. Newest entries first. Consolidate durable lessons into
`playbook.md` monthly.

---

<!-- Add entries below -->

# Lesson — HR & GA — 2026-07-05

**Trigger:** A claude.ai session (no repo write access at the time)
reviewed HR data and produced a handoff markdown file plus a draft xlsx,
to be picked up by a Claude Code session with real repo access. The
claude.ai session inferred org reporting lines and annotation meanings
from PDF/deck text extraction alone, without rendering the actual chart
images, and flagged 4 gaps as unresolved guesses.
**Source:** Self-observed, resuming this handoff in Claude Code with git
and `pdftoppm`/image-reading access.
**Lesson:** Text extraction from a slide deck does not reliably preserve
visual layout, an org chart's reporting lines and annotation placement
live in the shapes/lines, not the text run order. Rendering the actual
page as an image (`pdftoppm` + Read, `brew install poppler` if missing)
and reading it directly resolved 3 of the 4 "gaps needing human
confirmation" in minutes, versus guessing from linearized text. The
handoff-file pattern itself worked well: a single markdown file with a
numbered todo list, explicit "action for Claude Code session" callouts,
and a clear list of what's genuinely unresolved vs. inferred, made resuming
in a different session/tool with no shared conversation memory
straightforward.
**Applies to:** Any future cross-session handoff (claude.ai without repo
access to Claude Code with it), and any task involving reading org charts,
diagrams, or other spatially-laid-out content from a deck or PDF, render
the page as an image before trusting extracted text for structure.
**Status:** Logged

# Lesson — HR & GA — 2026-07-05

**Trigger:** Resuming this handoff, found Finance's Claude Code session
was concurrently active in the same repo with its own uncommitted changes
(modified `org/escalation-log.md`, `finance/staff-logs/*`, untracked
`data-inbox/finance/2026-*/` files).
**Source:** Self-observed.
**Lesson:** Multiple department Heads now have simultaneous real git
access to the same repo. Before staging/committing, check `git status` and
`git diff` for unstaged changes in shared files (`org/escalation-log.md`,
`org/kpi-dashboard.md`, `org/org-change-log.md`) that belong to another
department's in-progress work, and never `git add -A`/`git add .`, stage
only the files actually touched for this task so another session's
uncommitted work isn't swept into an unrelated commit.
**Applies to:** Every future session in this repo, now that multiple
Heads write concurrently, this is a standing concern, not a one-off.
**Status:** Logged

**Trigger:** Re-checking the Xendit.csv "VALUE-error rows" issue and the old-5101/5309-8201 retroactive cleanups against real FY2025+FY2026 journal data
**Source:** Self-observed
**Lesson:** Two assumptions carried over from the original handoff didn't survive contact with the actual data. (1) Xendit.csv's formula errors turned out to be confined to ~48 bolted-on workpaper columns, not the 22 real transaction fields, so the file was usable well before a fresh re-export arrived. (2) "5309/8201 historically mixed" turned out to be "5309 was never used at all," a much simpler split than a genuinely mixed pool would have needed. Also surfaced a MECE gap the original mapping missed: a third of old-5101's entries are Mailchimp, which belongs in 5201 Marketing, not the COGS split.
**Applies to:** Before declaring any data-quality or historical-cleanup item "blocked" or scoping a fix plan, actually query the real data for row-level counts and patterns rather than reasoning from the original description of the problem. The description may be stale, imprecise, or wrong about symmetry/scope.
**Status:** Logged

# Lesson — Finance — 2026-07-05

**Trigger:** Retroactive reclass and full historical CAC tagging approved by Barry
**Source:** Barry feedback
**Lesson:** Retroactive cleanups need the complete data export up front, sampled data (via search) is enough to design the mapping but not to execute it. Should ask for the full export as soon as a retroactive scope is even possible, rather than after approval.
**Applies to:** Any future retroactive reclass or historical data cleanup request.

**Trigger:** COGS/OpEx reclassification and CAC tagging requests surfaced during COA work
**Source:** Self-observed
**Lesson:** A "simple" chart-of-accounts cleanup can surface real cross-functional policy decisions (gross margin definition, CAC attribution methodology) that go beyond Finance's own call. Flagging these early and routing to FP&A/Tax & Compliance rather than deciding unilaterally kept the process honest. Barry confirmed that when cross-department clarification is needed (not just cross-work-stream within Finance), the relevant documents should be updated to formally ask the other Head, rather than assuming or deciding on their behalf.
**Applies to:** Any COA or reporting-structure change, always scan for downstream KPI/attribution implications before finalizing, and route genuinely cross-department questions to the relevant Head rather than deciding internally.
**Status:** Logged

# Lesson — Finance — 2026-07-05

**Trigger:** Reviewing a proposed COA restructure line by line
**Source:** Self-observed
**Lesson:** A handful of accounts had descriptions that directly contradicted their own account name or excluded the very thing they were named for (5202, 5207, 2207, 5315). These read like copy-paste errors that would have caused real misposting if applied as-is. Barry confirmed this check should also cover MECE (mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive) consistency across different accounts, not just internal name-vs-description consistency.
**Applies to:** Whenever reviewing any proposed COA or template change, check every account's name against its own description AND against every other account's scope for overlap or gaps, before treating it as ready to apply.

# Academic — Lessons Learned

Append-only log. Use `templates/lessons-learned.template.md` for each
entry. Newest entries first. Consolidate durable lessons into
`playbook.md` monthly.

---

<!-- Add entries below -->

# Lesson — Academic — 2026-07-13

**Trigger:** First real data drop (Sekolah Alkhairiyah usage/assessment
files) arrived directly from Barry in local Downloads, not
`data-inbox/academic/`, with full name/NISN/birthdate attached to two of
the five files, and the source's own "coded" student IDs turned out to
be name-derived, not real pseudonyms.
**Source:** Self-observed / Escalation outcome (`ESC-20260713-ACAD-02`).
**Lesson:** Never trust a source file's own "coded ID" as a real
pseudonym without checking how it was constructed, a code built from
name fragments (e.g. `AK-MUHAMMADDZ19`) is decodable by anyone with a
roster. Always regenerate a salted-hash pseudonym before anything
derived touches disk, and check every column, not just the obvious ones,
against `org/student-data-policy.md` rule 1's identifier list
(`Birthdate` is explicitly listed and is easy to miss since it doesn't
read as "personal data" the way a name does).
**Applies to:** every future `data-inbox/academic/` drop, and the
pseudonymization step in `academic/work-products/2026-07-13-alkhairiyah-usage-impact-analysis.md`
should be the template for how it's done.
**Status:** Logged.

# Lesson — Academic — 2026-07-13

**Trigger:** Sekolah Alkhairiyah usage-impact analysis found Smartick's
own internal progression metrics (Delta Level, Total Unit Dikuasai)
correlate strongly with usage (r=+0.42 to +0.56), while the one
external-ish outcome measure available showed no relationship (r=−0.05,
not significant).
**Source:** Self-observed.
**Lesson:** An internal platform-computed progression metric is not
independent evidence of learning impact when it is calculated from the
same session log as the usage measure being tested, the correlation is
close to mechanical. Any future causal or associational study must keep
internal-engine outcomes and external/independent outcomes in separate,
clearly labeled result sections, never blend them into one headline
number, or a mechanical relationship will get reported as if it were a
real effect.
**Applies to:** `academic/playbook.md`'s "Quarterly causal impact study"
default approach, folded in below.
**Status:** Folded into playbook (2026-07-13).

# Lesson — Academic — 2026-07-13

**Trigger:** Once individual-level TKA test scores were matched to usage
data, usage correlated with TKA Math (r=+0.24, p=0.005), a real,
statistically significant result on an independent academic test. Before
reporting it as a finding, checked whether usage predicted the TKA
Bahasa Indonesia score just as strongly, it did (r=+0.24), even though
Smartick has no Bahasa Indonesia content. That reframed the finding from
"usage improves math" to "usage correlates with a general
student-quality factor that isn't math-specific."
**Source:** Self-observed.
**Lesson:** Whenever a usage-vs-outcome study has access to more than one
outcome measure, and at least one plausibly should NOT move with the
product's usage (a subject or skill the product doesn't teach), always
run that as a placebo/falsification check before reporting a positive
result. A real product-specific effect should be stronger on the
in-scope outcome than the out-of-scope one, if they move equally, the
likely story is a shared confound (ability, motivation, home support),
not product impact.
**Applies to:** `academic/playbook.md`'s "Quarterly causal impact study"
default approach, folded in below. Use this pattern any time a second,
plausibly-unrelated outcome variable is available.
**Status:** Folded into playbook (2026-07-13).

# Lesson — Academic — 2026-07-13

**Trigger:** Reported a null finding (usage doesn't predict Asesmen Awal
gain) without having read the actual test content. Later reading the
real form (Barry supplied PDF exports) showed it's a general
reasoning/logic diagnostic, not a curriculum-content test, a construct
that plausibly has little to do with what Smartick usage builds. The
"no relationship" finding may be a construct mismatch, not evidence of
no impact.
**Source:** Self-observed.
**Lesson:** Before treating a null usage-vs-outcome result as a real
finding, verify what the outcome instrument actually measures, not just
that it exists and is "external." A null result on a test of the wrong
construct is not evidence of no effect, it's an uninformative
comparison. Get the actual instrument (questions, framing, scoring)
whenever feasible before finalizing an interpretation, not after.
**Applies to:** `academic/playbook.md`'s "Quarterly causal impact study"
default approach, folded in below.
**Status:** Folded into playbook (2026-07-13).

escalations.heading: Escalation — Finance — 2026-07-17
escalations.issue:
Retagging all past journals to the new COA surfaced that **June
2026 has 65 rows carrying Rp586,337,479 with a blank `Kode akun`** in the
source journal (`data-inbox/finance/2026-06/journal.csv`). These are real,
identifiable transactions, not noise: 27 salary payments (including Galih,
Hadi, Waitatiri, Barry), BNI Life insurance Rp180,797,800, BPJS
Ketenagakerjaan Rp21,443,277, BPJS Kesehatan Rp7,366,750, WMI office rent
Rp30,733,333, PPh 21 payments, e-materai. Each has a date, a description
and an amount, and their counterparts exist so the journal still balances
(Rp17,165,122,762 both sides), but they belong to no account and therefore
appear in no ledger or P&L line even though the cash actually moved.
Consequence: June 2026 cannot be closed, and no FY2026 statement can be
produced on an account basis, until they are coded. FY2025 has no such

Fira assigns the account codes. All 65 rows are listed
ready to fill in `finance/work-products/journal-retag-2025-2026-for-review.xlsx`,
sheet "Needs Your Review", with Old Code = "(none)". Finance can apply
them to the retag immediately once returned. Most look mechanical (salary
-> 5301, BPJS -> 5302/5303, rent -> 5305, insurance -> 5310), but Finance
should not guess account codes for Rp586m of real payroll and statutory
payments on a human officer's behalf.
escalations.whyNotTier1:
Needs a real person with knowledge of the actual
June 2026 transactions to assign codes; this is source-data completion,
not something the agentic side can infer safely at this materiality.
escalations.neededBy:
Before FY2026 books close or any FY2026 statement

escalations.issue:
Re-raising `ESC-20260705-COO-01` per the condition attached to
Barry's original decision ("if this is not enough, remember this
recommendation and bring it up again when the data is already present").
Real data has landed: Barry supplied 5 files 2026-07-13 (local Downloads,
Sekolah Alkhairiyah usage/assessment data). Two of them fail the
"student_id only, no personal data" bar: `Copy of Hasil_TKA_AlKhairiyah -
Hasil TKA.csv` carries full name (`Nama`), national student ID (`NISN`),
and birthdate (`Tanggal Lahir`) directly alongside test scores (149 real
rows); the three `Progres Murid Alkhairiyah - *.xlsx` files carry a
`Birthdate` column (explicitly a direct identifier per
`org/student-data-policy.md` rule 1) alongside usage/progression data
(781 real rows). Separately, the coded IDs already present in the source
files (`AK-<name-fragment><digits>`, e.g. `AK-MUHAMMADDZ19`) are not real
pseudonyms, they are derived from the student's own name and are likely
decodable by anyone with the school's class roster.
escalations.recommendation:
Handled this instance without waiting on Barry (data
was live and directly requested for analysis): raw files were not copied
into the repo, stay only in local Downloads; a proper non-decodable
pseudonym (`ALKH-<salted hash>`, crosswalk kept outside the repo, per
rule 1) replaced the source's name-derived codes; `Birthdate` was dropped
entirely before anything was written to disk; the TKA file's row-level
data was used only in-memory to compute a 3-row school-unit aggregate,
never persisted at student level. Detail in
`academic/work-products/2026-07-13-alkhairiyah-data/README.md` (moved
there from a first attempt at `data-inbox/academic/`, that path turned
out to be an org-level-only write path per `scripts/check-pr-scope.mjs`,
not something a department PR can populate). Going forward, recommend
whoever exports future drops (school, platform team) be told to strip
name/NISN/birthdate before the file reaches a Head's session at all,
this is the second real-data drop and the second time Academic has had
to build the stripping step itself rather than receive it pre-stripped.
escalations.whyNotTier1:
Per `academic/SYSTEM-PROMPT.md`, data-privacy
questions for children's data route to policy, not a Head's unilateral
call, and this is the exact re-raise condition Barry attached to his
original ruling.
escalations.neededBy:
Not blocking (already handled safely this time),
but before the next real data drop, so the stripping step happens before
it reaches a Head rather than after.
escalations.crossDept:
HR & GA (Legal & Compliance owns policy
interpretation), any department that later receives a real data-inbox
drop.
escalations.decision: noted, actually if possible you can just get the age, and mask all personal data so the children cannot be specifically identified. but, correct decision to never put personal/confidential data and upload it to your corpus or to our repository _(via dashboard, 2026-07-14)_

escalations.heading: Escalation — Sales — 2026-07-13
escalations.issue:
There is no single agreed customer-facing refund rule, and CS
answers refund requests live without one. The User Manual says refunds are
"annual plans only," but Finance's transaction review found real refund
amounts of Rp44,400 to Rp888,000, too small to be full annual-plan
refunds, so partial or prorated refunds are already happening in practice
(`finance/staff-logs/accounting-officer-log.md`, 2026-07-06; Product
Knowledge Base Section 3, "policy vs confirmed practice"). Product, Finance,
and CS have each separately noted that the three need to agree one rule,
but the gap has sat unresolved for 5 days with no escalation forcing the
decision. It now blocks certifying the Commercial Mechanics module (C3) of
the sales training curriculum
(`sales/deliverables/2026-07-13-sales-training-curriculum.md`), and more
importantly means CS may be telling different customers different things
about refunds today.
escalations.recommendation:
Convene a single decision on the customer-facing refund
rule (who is eligible, full vs prorated, over what window, routed how) so
CS can state it consistently and the curriculum can teach it. Until then,
CS trains and answers with only "refund requests route through Pedagogical
Support," asserting no eligibility or amount specifics. Finance's separate
refund-posting-accounting fix (2101 vs 4101) can proceed in parallel; this
escalation is about the customer-facing policy, not the ledger treatment.
escalations.whyNotTier1:
A refund policy is a company commitment across
three departments (Product owns the documented policy, Finance owns the
financial treatment, CS executes it with customers), not an operating
detail Sales can set alone.
escalations.neededBy:
Before C3 is certified and before the next refund
request is answered, since inconsistent answers are a live customer-trust
risk (compare the duplicate-charge billing-trust complaints in
`business/deliverables/b2c-game-plan-2026-07.md`).
escalations.crossDept:
Product (documented policy in the KB), Finance
(refund treatment and the open posting-rule cleanup), CS/Sales (executes
refunds with customers and trains on them).
escalations.decision: Sometimes decisions need to be made for aggravated customers who want to refund for their 1-month or 3-month subscription. In these cases, we make decisions to still give the refund. _(via dashboard, 2026-07-13)_

escalations.heading: Escalation — Academic — 2026-07-13
escalations.issue:
Sales (training curriculum Module C4, `sales/deliverables/2026-07-13-sales-training-curriculum.md`, PR #51, already merged and usable) and Marketing (`marketing/work-products/brand-positioning-workplan.md`
Workstream 4) are both asking Academic to confirm or supply learning-outcome
and confidence proof points for prospect/parent-facing claims. All three
figures in play, NPS ~+61, perceived effectiveness 8.18/10, and the
Sekolah Alkhairiyah one-year reflection, come from Marketing's
`marketing/work-products/voice-of-customer-2026-07.md`, sourced from real
survey data Barry supplied 2026-07-10. None of it is Academic-generated:
Academic has not run a causal outcome study and `data-inbox/academic/` is
still empty. Two specific risks: (1) NPS and perceived-effectiveness are
self-reported satisfaction/perception metrics from a self-selected,
non-representative sample (Marketing's own caveat in that file), not
independently verified learning-outcome data, citing them as proof of
learning outcomes overstates what they show; (2) the same Alkhairiyah
dataset that supports the confidence-gain narrative also documents real,
recurring difficulty-calibration complaints (questions arriving above
grade level or before material was taught, causing frustration), which is
literally the worry Module C4 exists to defuse. A pitch that cites only
the positive quotes risks a rep getting caught flat-footed if a prospect's
actual experience matches the documented negative.
escalations.recommendation:
Approve one shared, Barry-signed-off external
proof-points reference that both Sales and Marketing draw from (instead of
Academic answering each department ad hoc and risking drift), with three
ground rules: (a) NPS and perceived-effectiveness always cited as
parent-reported satisfaction/perception from a self-selected survey
sample, never worded as a measured learning-outcome effect; (b) the
Alkhairiyah case is a legitimate, citable proof point for adaptive
difficulty and confidence-building, but Sales's C4 role-play should
explicitly rehearse the honest edge case (occasional above-level
questions, connectivity, short-session timing) rather than only the
positive quotes; (c) until Academic runs its own causal outcome study, no
external claim should say Academic "proved" or "measured" a learning
effect, only that pedagogy design and early customer/school signal are
directionally consistent.
escalations.whyNotTier1:
per `academic/SYSTEM-PROMPT.md`, any finding
published outside the org (parents, schools, marketing claims) and any
cross-department use of impact data is Tier 2. This is both at once, two
departments are about to put externally-facing claims in front of real
prospects and school buyers using data neither Academic nor Marketing has
independently verified for outcome claims.
escalations.neededBy:
Not blocking today (Sales's curriculum is already
merged and usable per Faiz), but should not sit long, C4 is already
trainable and Marketing's Workstream 4 is only "partially unblocked"
pending this.
escalations.crossDept:
Sales (training curriculum C4/C5), Marketing
(brand-positioning Workstream 4). Both are currently waiting on this exact
confirmation from Academic.
escalations.decision: there is already another analysis done by academic team that shows there is a strong positive correlation of Smartick activity to nilai TKA, please just work around this narrative. sure, there is a caveat behind, but work around it. _(via dashboard, 2026-07-14)_

(1) Route the difficulty-mismatch hypothesis to
whoever owns the Day-0 activation nudge work (`ESC-20260708-BUS-02`,
Product/CS per Business's routing) as a candidate root cause worth
testing against, alongside their existing hypotheses. Marketing is not

Sales (same), Product (activation root-cause testing), Academic
(Alkhairiyah's data includes a pre-built qualitative thematic-coding

escalations.heading: Escalation — Marketing — 2026-07-10
escalations.issue:

growth. Answer: not computable, no registration or subscription record
anywhere in the repo (checked Business's full-funnel analysis and
tracker work, Sales's pipeline docs) carries a channel/source field, so
real CAC-by-channel cannot be calculated. The only proxy available is
the NAY campaign's own budget structure: 63% of its Rp27,331,000 budget
goes to one offline booth reaching an estimated 100+ visitors
(~Rp170,000/visitor), versus 11% on paid digital, while owned/organic
channels (45.2K IG followers) carry most reach at near-zero cash cost.
Full analysis: `marketing/work-products/channel-strategy-2026-07.md`.
escalations.recommendation:
Add a channel/source field at registration (e.g.
UTM-derived or a manual "how did you hear about us" field) so a future
pass can compute real channel CAC and conversion. This is a Product
build (registration flow) and overlaps directly with Finance's
already-in-progress CAC-tag mechanism work
(`finance/daily-checkins/2026-07-06.md`), recommend scoping it as one
joint piece of work across Product and Finance rather than two separate
asks landing on each separately. Lower-cost interim step: once the NAY
campaign closes (2026-07-12), do a manual channel post-mortem from
whatever ad-platform dashboards, Shopee sales, and booth registration
counts show directly, this is the first real spend-to-signup dataset
Marketing will have.
escalations.whyNotTier1:
Building the field is a Product engineering
task Marketing cannot do itself, and it needs to be coordinated with
Finance's parallel CAC-tagging work rather than built twice.
escalations.neededBy:
Not urgent this week, but before any future
paid-acquisition spend increase is proposed, without it the same "which
channel" question will be unanswerable again next time it's asked.
escalations.crossDept:
Product (owns the registration flow build),
Finance (CAC-tag mechanism should be scoped jointly, not duplicated).
escalations.decision: This is a hard problem now. We cannot add an event tracker that can reliably track the source of sales. What can we alternatively do to tackle this problem? Should we just ask all the subscribers one by one of when do they first know Smartick? and what makes them subscribing? _(via dashboard, 2026-07-12)_

escalations.heading: Escalation — Finance — 2026-07-09
escalations.issue:
Barry asked Finance to break Xendit revenue into prepaid
revenue, subscription revenue already recognized/used, and payment
gateway fee. The gateway fee and gross/net cash flow are fully computable
from Xendit data (Rp23,456,516 total fee, Feb 2025-Jun 2026, see
`finance/work-products/xendit-revenue-breakdown-2025-2026.md`), but the
prepaid-vs-recognized split needs per-subscription plan data (plan
length, start date, pause/cuti status) that the payment gateway export
does not contain. Checked whether transaction amount could substitute:
it can't, top price points don't fall on clean multiples of a base price,
consistent with Business's ~35.68% average discount finding
(ESC-20260706-BUS-02), so amount-based inference risks a materially wrong
deferred-revenue split.
escalations.recommendation:
Point Finance to the subscription/billing system's
data source (plan type, start date, status per subscription), likely
owned by Product or wherever the billing backend lives. Once available,
Accounting Officer can build a proper amortization schedule that also
accounts for "cuti" pauses extending the recognition period.
escalations.whyNotTier1:
Requires access to a data source Finance doesn't
currently have and doesn't know the owner of, not something resolvable
from data already in `data-inbox/finance/`.
escalations.neededBy:
Before the prepaid/recognized revenue split can
be completed, no hard deadline otherwise.
escalations.crossDept:
Likely Product (billing/subscription system
ownership unconfirmed from Finance's view, Barry to confirm who owns it).

escalations.heading: Escalation — Business — 2026-07-08
escalations.issue:
Full registrant database analysis (43,837 real registrants,
junk rows excluded) found 41,137 never converted to paid, and of those,
56.3% completed zero sessions ever and another 32.0% completed only 1-2
sessions out of a 7-day trial. 88.3% of every lost registrant barely
touched the product. This is roughly two orders of magnitude larger than
the 656-record churn backlog flagged in `ESC-20260706-BUS-03`. Full
detail: `business/deliverables/b2c-trial-activation-priority-2026-07-08.md`.
escalations.recommendation:
(1) Build and test a Day-0 activation nudge, the
existing nurture playbook starts at Day 2, nothing runs same-day as
registration. (2) Manually sample-contact a small batch of zero-session
registrants to learn the real reason before building a larger campaign on
a guess.
escalations.whyNotTier1:
Needs real Product/engineering time to build and
ship a Day-0 flow, and real people to make sample contact calls/messages.
Business can size the problem and propose the test design, not execute
either.
escalations.neededBy:
Before any further top-of-funnel acquisition
spend increase, since that spend is currently filling a funnel that loses
88% of what enters it before product quality or price ever come into play.
escalations.crossDept:
Product (Day-0 activation flow, product
analytics to diagnose the drop-off), Sales/CS (sample outreach
execution).
escalations.decision: Yes, please. Do it, build a plan of day-0 activation nudge, improve the playbook, and devise contact sampling mechanism and its evaluation metrics whether we should build a larger campaign or not. _(via dashboard, 2026-07-12)_
